target.title: feat(tools/write): add append mode for agent writes
target.body:
## Summary

Revives and rebases the closed append-mode work from #75549 for the canonical data-loss issue #40001.

The `write` tool currently overwrites existing files. OpenClaw has an append-only wrapper for memory-flush runs, but ordinary agent turns, cron skills, and monitor-and-react workflows do not have a first-class append path. This PR adds a backwards-compatible optional `append: true` parameter to `write` so agents can append without synthesizing a read-concatenate-overwrite sequence.

When `append` is omitted or false, existing overwrite behavior is unchanged.

## What changed

- Adds optional `append?: boolean` handling for host/workspace and sandbox write operations.
- Preserves existing workspace/root guard behavior.
- Serializes concurrent appends through the existing file mutation queue.
- Preserves validation behavior for blank paths and invalid content.
- Surfaces sandbox I/O failures except for missing-file cases used to create-on-append.
- Honors aborted append calls before entering the mutation queue.
- Adds focused regression coverage for append-create, append-accumulate, append-false-overwrites, validation, and sandbox/workspace behavior.

## Validation

Run in an isolated checkout on current `origin/main`:

- `corepack pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.read.host-tilde-expansion.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.params.test.ts`
  - passed: `10 files / 170 tests`
- `git diff --check`
- `corepack pnpm lint:core`
  - passed: `0 warnings / 0 errors`

## Notes

This PR intentionally keeps the API small and backwards-compatible. It does not remove or relax the existing memory-flush append-only wrapper; it gives ordinary `write` callers an explicit append path instead of relying on prompt discipline or destructive read/concatenate/write patterns.

Fixes #40001.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** The agent write tool supports `append: true` without overwriting the existing file, while still using the workspace-root guard for workspace-only host writes.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw source checkout on Linux using the actual `createHostWorkspaceWriteTool` implementation against a temporary workspace on the real filesystem.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx` terminal smoke that created the host workspace write tool, wrote `first line`, reran the same write tool with `append: true` for `second line`, then read the resulting file.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal capture from the smoke:

  ```text
  workspace: $TMPDIR/openclaw-write-append-proof-.../workspace
  tool result: append:true preserved existing content and added the second line
  file content:
  first line
  second line
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The final file retained the original content and appended the new content in order; the append path did not truncate or replace the file.
- **What was not tested:** No live Gateway session was exercised; validation used the real tool implementation in an isolated local checkout plus targeted regression/type checks.

target.title: fix(qqbot): add TCP keepalive + heartbeat interval logging to prevent silent connection drops
target.body:
### Problem

**Messages delayed by up to 9 minutes due to silent WebSocket connection failures.**

On 2026-05-04 07:13, a user sent a message via QQBot. The message was not received until 07:22:18 — **9 minutes of delay**. During this entire period, the OpenClaw gateway showed no errors, no reconnection attempts, and no heartbeat failures. The connection appeared healthy but was in fact dead.

**What actually happened:**
- The QQ WebSocket connection was open from 07:04:03 to 07:22:16 (18 minutes, no drops logged)
- At 07:22:16, the connection abruptly closed with code `1006` (abnormal close, no close frame)
- Immediately after reconnecting at 07:22:18, all queued messages arrived at once
- **Conclusion:** The connection silently died well before 07:13, but neither the client nor the QQ server detected it. Messages were buffered server-side and only delivered after the client reconnect.

**Why no detection?** The existing QQBot gateway implementation has two gaps:
1. **No TCP keepalive** — The `ws` library was instantiated without `setKeepAlive`, meaning the OS-level TCP connection is not probed. Network middleboxes (routers, NAT, firewalls) silently drop idle connections without sending RST/FIN.
2. **No heartbeat timeout detection** — If the server sends a `HEARTBEAT_ACK` on time, the client assumes the connection is healthy. But if the `HEARTBEAT_ACK` is lost in transit (because the underlying TCP path is dead), the client never notices. There is no timeout or ACK validation.

**Root cause:** The 1006 abnormal close indicates the connection was terminated without a proper WebSocket close frame — likely at the TCP or network layer (NAT timeout, silent packet drop). The client had no way to detect this before receiving a message, because there was no lower-level keepalive probe and no heartbeat ACK timeout.

### Solution

**Three changes in `extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/gateway-connection.ts`:**

#### 1. TCP Keepalive (Prevention)
```typescript
const socket = (ws as unknown as { _socket: Socket | null })._socket;
if (socket) {
  socket.setKeepAlive(true, 30_000);  // probe every 30s
  socket.setTimeout(60_000);          // 60s with no data → timeout
  socket.on("timeout", () => {
    log?.warn?.(`WebSocket socket timeout — closing and reconnecting`);
    ws.close();  // triggers existing reconnect flow
  });
}
```

- `setKeepAlive(true, 30_000)` — OS sends TCP keepalive probes every 30 seconds, keeping NAT mappings alive and detecting dead connections at the transport layer
- `setTimeout(60_000)` — if no data (including keepalive ACK) is received for 60 seconds, the socket is considered dead and closed, triggering an immediate reconnect

#### 2. Heartbeat Interval Logging (Debugging)
```typescript
const interval = d.heartbeat_interval;
this.ctx.log?.info(`Heartbeat interval: ${interval}ms (${(interval / 1000).toFixed(1)}s)`);
```

- Logs the heartbeat interval sent by the QQ server on every connect/resume
- Previously unknown: QQ server uses **41.25 seconds**, not a round number
- Critical for diagnosing heartbeat-related issues in the future

#### 3. WebSocket Compression
```typescript
perMessageDeflate: true
```
- Standard best practice for `ws` library; reduces bandwidth for JSON payloads

### Verification

- **7 new unit tests** for keepalive and heartbeat logging logic
- **50 existing tests** pass (message-queue, outbound-dispatch, inbound-pipeline, envelope-stage)
- **Live deployment test:** After deploying, TCP socket timeout was correctly detected on a dead connection at 10:38:48, triggering automatic reconnect. Heartbeat interval logged as `41250ms (41.3s)`.

### Expected Impact

- **Before:** Dead connections go undetected for minutes. Users see message delays of 5-10 minutes. Only a manual restart or the next server-side close triggers reconnect.
- **After:** Dead connections detected within 60 seconds via TCP keepalive timeout. Automatic reconnect restores message delivery. Heartbeat interval visible in logs for troubleshooting.

target.title: fix(cli): exit after hooks inspection output
target.body:
## Summary

- port #70255 onto current main
- keep hooks list/info/check/enable/disable and bare `hooks` as one-shot CLI commands
- keep relay plus deprecated install/update behavior unchanged
- add the missing changelog entry

## Why

Plugin diagnostics can load third-party hook plugins. If a plugin leaves a timer or other handle open, the hooks CLI can print output but keep the process alive. These hooks commands are inspection or one-shot mutation commands, so they should exit after successful output.

Supersedes #70255.

## Tests

- `pnpm test src/cli/hooks-cli.test.ts src/plugins/status.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:no-conflict-markers`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/cli/hooks-cli.ts src/cli/hooks-cli.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: One-shot `openclaw hooks list --json` should print its output and exit instead of hanging after hook/plugin diagnostics leave open handles.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, using this PR branch and the real CLI path, not a unit test.

target.title: fix(discord): preserve target intent during normalization
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Discord-like explicit DM targets could be normalized through the id-like target path without preserving the intended direct-message kind.
- Why it matters: A Discord target that is syntactically a user/direct target should stay routed as a user/direct target instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference.
- What changed: Shared target-kind inference now lives in `target-normalization`, and both outbound target resolution and cron delivery pass the inferred kind into id-like plugin resolution.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No Discord runtime sending behavior, config schema, plugin manifest, or user-facing docs changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Explicit Discord direct-message targets such as `user:<id>` preserve inferred user/direct intent when id-like target resolution runs for outbound and cron delivery.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Windows/PowerShell, Discord plugin configured with a real bot token and a redacted explicit `user:<id>` target.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran local OpenClaw Discord outbound/cron delivery paths against an explicit `user:<id>` Discord target using the patched branch, then compared the live delivery result with the new resolver/cron coverage.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Existing live-result report from @AScriver after the local Discord run, with private IDs and tokens redacted:

```text
Scenario: Discord outbound/cron delivery to an explicit user:<id> target
Result: delivered to the intended DM recipient and preserved explicit user target intent instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference
Coverage alignment: matches the targeted resolver and cron tests added in this PR
```

- Observed result after fix: Discord delivered to the intended DM recipient, and the explicit user/direct target intent was preserved instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference.
- What was not tested: Raw Discord token and private IDs are not published; no additional non-Discord channels were tested live.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Source-level before-state showed the id-like resolver did not receive inferred `preferredKind` for outbound/cron paths; the targeted regression tests cover that missing handoff.
## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: id-like target resolution received only the raw target string, so the plugin resolver could lose the caller's inferred direct-message intent after normalization.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered id-like fallback and directory inference separately, but not propagation of inferred DM/user kind into plugin id-like resolution.
- Contributing context (if known): Discord target parsing can distinguish user/direct targets before the generic id-like normalization path runs.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/infra/outbound/target-resolver.test.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-target.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: inferred direct-message/user kind is passed into id-like plugin target resolution.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it validates the shared resolver seam and cron delivery call path without needing a live Discord bot.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Discord-targeted delivery paths preserve explicit direct-message intent more reliably when resolving id-like targets. No config or command syntax changes.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Discord DM-like target -> generic id-like resolution -> kind may default away from user/direct intent

After:
Discord DM-like target -> infer user/direct kind -> id-like resolution receives preferredKind=user -> direct target preserved
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows, PowerShell
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout, Node/pnpm from workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord target routing logic
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Resolve a plugin id-like target whose channel messaging adapter infers `direct` chat type.
2. Resolve a cron delivery target for an explicit DM-like target.
3. Assert the id-like resolver receives `preferredKind: "user"` and preserves the returned target.

### Expected

- The id-like plugin resolver receives the inferred user/direct kind.
- Cron delivery preserves explicit DM intent after target normalization.

### Actual

- Verified by the new focused tests.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Targeted validation:

```bash
pnpm test src/infra/outbound/target-resolver.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-target.test.ts
pnpm check:changed
```

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: local Discord outbound/cron delivery to a redacted explicit `user:<id>` target was reported after this patch; target resolver and cron delivery target unit/seam coverage also passed locally.
- Edge cases checked: id-like raw targets continue passing `preferredKind: "group"`; explicit DM-like targets pass `preferredKind: "user"`.
- What I did **not** verify: raw Discord token/private IDs are intentionally not published; no additional non-Discord channels were tested live.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: shared target kind inference could affect non-Discord channels that expose `inferTargetChatType`.
  - Mitigation: the helper preserves existing fallback behavior, maps `channel` to `group` where directory lookup expects groups, and adds focused coverage for the new id-like propagation path.

target.title: fix(plugins): supplement ClawHub artifact metadata
target.body:
## Summary

- Accept current ClawHub artifact resolver responses where `artifact` is the package artifact summary shape.
- When the resolver response has a legacy zip artifact but no archive verification metadata, load the version metadata and use its `sha256hash` or `files[]` for verification.
- Add a regression test for the current `clawhub:zeroapi@3.8.9` response shape.

## Why

The ClawHub artifact endpoint can return a legacy zip artifact summary while the install verification metadata is only present on the version endpoint. OpenClaw rejected that package as missing verification metadata, even though the package was valid and installable.

## Tests

- `pnpm test src/plugins/clawhub.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/infra/clawhub.ts src/plugins/clawhub.ts src/plugins/clawhub.test.ts && git diff --check`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Real-world validation

- `HOME=$tmp/home OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=$tmp/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=$tmp/openclaw.json pnpm openclaw plugins install clawhub:zeroapi@3.8.9 --dangerously-force-unsafe-install`
- Result: installed `zeroapi-router` from ClawHub in an isolated temp state.

## AI-assisted

Yes. I used Codex and reviewed the changed code and tests.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Installing a ClawHub legacy zip plugin must verify archive metadata using the full version metadata path when the artifact resolver only returns summary metadata.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, using this PR branch and an isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_HOME`, installing the live `clawhub:zeroapi` package.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the real CLI install path from the PR branch: `OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-real-proof-NSAg8l pnpm openclaw plugins install clawhub:zeroapi --force`.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the real CLI command:

```text
OPENCLAW_HOME:/tmp/openclaw-real-proof-NSAg8l
exit:0
> openclaw@2026.5.4 openclaw /Users/doruk/Desktop/Asuman/.worktrees/openclaw-clawhub-artifact-metadata
> node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins install clawhub:zeroapi --force

Resolving clawhub:zeroapi...
ClawHub code-plugin zeroapi@3.8.12 channel=community verification=source-linked
Compatibility: pluginApi=>=2026.5.2 minGateway=2026.5.2
ClawHub package "zeroapi" is community...
Downloading plugin zeroapi@3.8.12 from ClawHub...
Extracting ... zeroapi-3.8.12.tgz...
Plugin manifest id "zeroapi-router" differs from npm package name "zeroapi"; using manifest id as the config key.
Installing to /tmp/openclaw-real-proof-NSAg8l/.openclaw/extensions/zeroapi-router...
Installed plugin: zeroapi-router
Restart the gateway to load plugins.
stderr_tail: [plugins] zeroapi-config.json not found. Run /zeroapi to configure.
```

- Observed result after fix: The live ClawHub plugin install resolved version metadata, downloaded and extracted the plugin, and exited with status 0 in a fresh temporary OpenClaw home.
- What was not tested: I did not restart a full gateway from the temporary install. The proof covers the resolver/install path changed by this PR, while focused tests cover metadata fallback behavior.

target.title: fix: prune all history images when current turn has fresh attachments
target.body:
## Problem

When sending a new image for analysis, stale image data from previous conversation turns can contaminate the model's interpretation of the current image. Reported in #66702.

## Solution

- Add `HistoryImagePruneOptions` interface with `preserveRecentCompletedTurns` parameter
- Thread option through `resolvePruneBeforeIndex` → `pruneProcessedHistoryImages` → caller in `attempt.ts`
- When current turn has fresh images, pass `preserveRecentCompletedTurns: 0` to strip all historical image data
- No new images: existing conservative default (preserve 3 turns) applies

Backward-compatible. Existing tests unchanged.

Fixes #66702

target.title: feat(telegram): add acknowledgement sticker support
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Telegram does not support streaming text, so users get no immediate interactive feedback while OpenClaw generates a response.
- Why it matters: Ack reactions help, but stickers are more visible and expressive for Telegram conversations.
- What changed: Added configurable Telegram ack stickers with scope control, optional silent delivery, optional removal after reply, config schema/docs, SDK feedback support, and Telegram tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Existing ack reaction behavior remains supported; this does not add text streaming to Telegram or change default Telegram access policy.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): Telegram lacks streaming text support, so acknowledgement feedback is handled through channel feedback primitives.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/agents/identity.test.ts`
  - `extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts`
  - `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.ack-sticker.test.ts`
  - `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Telegram resolves ackSticker config, sends the configured sticker before dispatching the agent turn, and optionally removes it after reply.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It covers config resolution, channel feedback shape, Telegram send behavior, and dispatch cleanup without requiring live Telegram credentials.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing ack reaction tests covered reaction behavior only.
- If no new test is added, why not: New tests are added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Telegram users can configure `ackSticker` globally, per account, per direct/group chat, or per topic. When enabled, OpenClaw sends a Telegram sticker acknowledgement before generating the response. Existing ack reactions continue to work.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Telegram message -> OpenClaw agent loop -> Telegram final reply

After:
Telegram message -> ack sticker -> OpenClaw agent loop -> Telegram final reply -> optional ack sticker cleanup
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Telegram may call `sendSticker` and optionally delete that sticker message. This uses the existing configured Telegram bot token and existing channel access controls; no new credential source or sender authorization path is added.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22, pnpm
- Model/provider: Not required for unit coverage
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json5
{
  channels: {
    telegram: {
      enabled: true,
      ackSticker: {
        fileId: "CAAC...",
        scope: "direct",
        silent: true,
        removeAfterReply: true,
      },
    },
  },
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure Telegram `ackSticker.fileId` with a sendable Telegram sticker file id.
2. Start the OpenClaw gateway with Telegram enabled.
3. Send a DM to the Telegram bot.

### Expected

- Telegram sends the configured acknowledgement sticker before the agent response.
- The agent response is delivered normally.
- If `removeAfterReply` is enabled, the acknowledgement sticker is deleted after the final reply.

### Actual

- Unit and config tests pass for the implemented behavior.
- Live Telegram verification was attempted locally but blocked by local gateway token/env setup issues, not by the ackSticker implementation.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets

- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Validation run:

```sh
pnpm test src/agents/identity.test.ts
pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts
pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.ack-sticker.test.ts
pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts
pnpm config:schema:check
pnpm config:channels:check
pnpm config:docs:gen
pnpm config:docs:check
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <changed-ts-files>
git diff --check
```

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - Config schema accepts ackSticker fields.
  - Channel feedback carries ackSticker configuration.
  - Telegram send path sends stickers and respects notification options.
  - Telegram dispatch path can clean up ack stickers after reply.
  - Docs/generated config baseline are updated.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Scope resolution mirrors ack reaction behavior.
  - Sticker cleanup is best-effort.
  - Ack reaction remains available and can be disabled independently.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Full live Telegram round-trip with a real bot after local launchd token setup issues.
  - Broad full-suite CI locally.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: Existing configs continue to work. Users can opt in by adding `channels.telegram.ackSticker` or `messages.ackSticker`.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Invalid Telegram sticker `file_id` causes the acknowledgement sticker send to fail.
  - Mitigation: Ack sticker send failure is isolated from final reply delivery.
- Risk: Sticker deletion can fail due to Telegram permissions or timing.
  - Mitigation: Cleanup is best-effort and does not block final response delivery.

target.title: Fix/cron duplicate name validation
target.body:
Fixes #76160

\`openclaw cron add\` and \`cron edit\` now enforce unique job names, preventing silent duplicate-name acceptance that could lead to dual-fire risk when both jobs remain enabled.

## Problem

Previously, the cron service silently accepted jobs with duplicate names:
- Two \`cron add\` calls with the same name created two distinct rows with different IDs
- \`cron edit\` could rename a job onto an existing name
- Default \`cron list\` only showed one job when their \`enabled\` states differed, hiding the divergence
- If both jobs remained enabled, they would fire twice per cycle with potentially divergent prompts

## Solution

Added name uniqueness validation in the service layer:
- **\`cron.add\`**: Checks for existing job with the same name before creating
- **\`cron.update\`**: Checks for name collision when renaming (allows no-op self-rename)
- **Disabled jobs**: Included in uniqueness check to prevent the exact scenario from the bug report
- **Error messages**: Clear, actionable format with existing job ID and removal instructions

## Implementation

- \`src/cron/service/ops.ts\`: Added \`findJobByName()\` checks in \`add()\` and \`update()\`
- \`src/cron/service/jobs.ts\`: Added \`findJobByName()\` helper function
- \`src/cron/service.duplicate-names.test.ts\`: Comprehensive test suite (13 test cases)

All entry points (CLI, agent tool, gateway RPC) funnel through the service layer, so validation is enforced everywhere.

## Testing

Added dedicated test suite covering:
- Adding jobs with duplicate names (enabled and disabled)
- Renaming jobs to existing names (enabled and disabled)
- Edge cases (case sensitivity, special characters, empty names)
- No-op renames (renaming to same name)
- Error message validation

## Breaking Changes

None. This is a bug fix that prevents invalid state. Operators who were accidentally creating duplicate names will now receive clear error messages with instructions to resolve the conflict.

## Related

- Supersedes closed PR #76180
- Found during production config audit in markthebest12/openclaw-infra#1305" --base main

target.title: fix(agents): pause yielded main-session runs
target.body:
## Summary

Extend the subagent yield-pause fix from [6f3b5f86](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/6f3b5f8666f3a3877b62d9019b8842455fdf62e1) to top-level (non-subagent) sessions.

When a main session ends via the `sessions_yield` tool, the runner already emits a lifecycle event with `yielded: true` and `livenessState: "paused"`. The runner's terminal lifecycle metadata, the agent-job snapshot cache, and the agent.wait dedupe entry all propagate those flags generically, so subagent and non-subagent runs benefit equally from those parts of 6f3b5f86.

The gap is in `gateway/session-lifecycle-state.ts`. `deriveGatewaySessionLifecycleSnapshot` does not look at the `yielded` flag, so the session entry is persisted with `status: \"done\"` even though the runner has queued a post-yield continuation that drains on the next turn. Dashboards, restart recovery, and follow-up turns then treat the session as finished and race the continuation, producing the in-context overflow and out-of-order events the user reports.

## Why the same fix needs to apply to the top-level session

The subagent registry tracks active subagent runs in memory and uses `pauseReason: \"sessions_yield\"` to keep yielded entries in a paused state instead of marking them complete. The main session has no equivalent in-memory registry; its lifecycle state is persisted directly through `persistGatewaySessionLifecycleEvent` into the on-disk session entry. The gap shows up in three places:

1. The persisted session entry says `status: \"done\"`, so `sessions_list`, `sessions.changed` broadcasts, and dashboard UI all show the session as finished.
2. `main-session-restart-recovery` is keyed off `entry.abortedLastRun` and `entry.status === \"running\"`. With status=done it leaves the entry alone, but on a clean restart-mid-yield the yielded session can also end up `abortedLastRun=true` (the cleaned-lock path), at which point recovery would force a `[System] Your previous turn was interrupted...` resume that races the queued continuation.
3. A new user message arrives at a session that is internally paused but externally \"done\" — the gateway dispatches a fresh agent.run, and the queued steered yield-interrupt drains alongside the new user input, producing out-of-order assistant text.

## Shape of the change

- Recognize the lifecycle `yielded` flag in `deriveGatewaySessionLifecycleSnapshot` and derive `status: \"paused\"` with `pauseReason: \"sessions_yield\"` instead of `done`. Yielded short-circuits before the `aborted`/`stopReason` branches so partial classifications cannot mask the paused state.
- Clear `pauseReason` on the next lifecycle `start` event so a fresh run reactivates the session, mirroring how `subagent-registry-lifecycle.ts` clears `pauseReason` on completion.
- Add `\"paused\"` to `SessionRunStatus` in both `gateway/session-utils.types.ts` and `agents/tools/sessions-helpers.ts`, and add `pauseReason?: \"sessions_yield\"` to `SessionEntry` and `GatewaySessionRow`.
- Surface `pauseReason` on the `sessions.changed` broadcast snapshot and the `loadGatewaySessionRow` builder so consumers (UI, sessions_list tool) see the paused state.
- Tighten `isTerminalSessionStatus` so the type predicate stays accurate after \"paused\" joins the union (paused is not terminal).
- Skip `main-session-restart-recovery` for entries with `pauseReason: \"sessions_yield\"` so they are not re-prompted with the generic interrupted-turn message.

The runner-level changes (yieldDetected -> livenessState=paused/yielded=true) and the gateway agent-job/agent-wait-dedupe propagation that 6f3b5f86 already added are unchanged. This PR plugs the missing seam between those generic signals and the persisted session entry.

## Test coverage

- `src/gateway/session-lifecycle-state.test.ts`:
  - `marks yielded lifecycle end events as paused with sessions_yield reason` (persisted patch).
  - `treats yielded as paused even when aborted is set` (yield short-circuit).
  - `clears pauseReason when a fresh run starts on a paused session` (start-phase reactivation).
  - Existing terminal-mapping tests updated to include explicit `pauseReason: undefined` on non-yield ends.
- `src/agents/main-session-restart-recovery.test.ts`:
  - `skips sessions paused via sessions_yield instead of forcing a resume`.
  - `does not mark sessions with sessions_yield pauseReason as restart-aborted from cleaned locks`.

## Local validation

- `pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:core:test`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` — clean.
- `pnpm test src/gateway/session-lifecycle-state.test.ts src/agents/main-session-restart-recovery.test.ts src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts src/config/sessions.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-list-tool.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.create-event.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent-wait-dedupe.test.ts src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-output.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/sessions-yield.orchestration.test.ts` — all green (235 tests).
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check`, `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs`, `pnpm format:docs:check`, `pnpm check:changelog-attributions` — all clean on changed files.

## Test plan

- [ ] CI green on \`fix(agents): pause yielded main-session runs\`.
- [ ] Manual smoke: trigger `sessions_yield` from a main-session conversation; confirm the session entry persists `status: paused` and `pauseReason: sessions_yield`, and that a follow-up turn flips back to `running` with `pauseReason: undefined`.
- [ ] Restart-recovery smoke: kill the gateway while a main session is paused via yield; confirm restart-recovery skips it instead of re-prompting with the interrupted-turn message.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Before this PR, when a top-level (non-subagent) embedded `openclaw` run ended via the `sessions_yield` tool, the runner emitted `yielded=true` and `livenessState=paused` but the gateway treated it as a clean completion and wrote `status=done`. The session displayed as finished while awaiting a queued post-yield continuation, racing dashboards, restart-recovery, and follow-up turns. After this PR, the lifecycle persistence derives `status=paused` with `pauseReason=sessions_yield`, restart-recovery skips paused sessions, and `SessionRunStatus` adds `"paused"` so consumers can distinguish a yielded run from a completed one.
- **Real environment tested**: live OpenClaw gateway on rh-bot.lan, pid 65734, build SHA 23804e610c (upgrade-v2026.5.28 with this PR cherry-picked). The deployed `dist/server-chat-*.js` and `dist/main-session-restart-recovery-*.js` chunks carry the new `pauseReason`/`sessions_yield` logic; the proof script imports and executes those exact bundle bytes.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  # On rh-bot.lan, confirm deployed bundles carry the PR logic:
  grep -c 'sessions_yield\|pauseReason' \
    /Users/alexm/.openclaw/openclaw/dist/main-session-restart-recovery-CirHJY3p.js
  # -> 1 (the new shouldSkipMainRecovery branch)

  grep -c 'sessions_yield\|pauseReason' \
    /Users/alexm/.openclaw/openclaw/dist/server-chat-D9hpq-gf.js
  # -> non-zero (new deriveGatewaySessionLifecycleSnapshot branches)

  # Drive the deployed code with three scenarios:
  node /tmp/proof-75662-amittell.mjs
  ```
  Proof script (`/tmp/proof-75662-amittell.mjs`) extracts the deployed
  `deriveGatewaySessionLifecycleSnapshot` from `server-chat-D9hpq-gf.js` via
  `vm.runInContext`, dynamically imports `main-session-restart-recovery-CirHJY3p.js`
  to grab the exported `markRestartAbortedMainSessions`, seeds a temp
  state-dir with one yielded and one non-yielded `running` entry, and asserts
  three behaviors:
  1. Lifecycle `phase=end` with `yielded=true` -> `status=paused`,
     `pauseReason=sessions_yield`, `abortedLastRun=false`.
  2. Same entry passed to restart-recovery is SKIPPED (no `abortedLastRun`
     write, `updatedAt` unchanged).
  3. Sibling running entry without `pauseReason` is marked
     `abortedLastRun=true` (existing behavior preserved).
  Plus a guard check: lifecycle `phase=error` with `yielded=true` returns
  `status=failed` with NO `pauseReason` leak.
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  === PR #75662 real-behavior proof ===
  host=rh-bot.lan pid=84884 node=v25.8.2
  dist=/Users/alexm/.openclaw/openclaw/dist

  [1] lifecycle bundle: /Users/alexm/.openclaw/openclaw/dist/server-chat-D9hpq-gf.js
  [1a] yielded terminal -> {"updatedAt":1780285520789,"status":"paused","startedAt":1780285519789,"endedAt":1780285520789,"runtimeMs":1000,"abortedLastRun":false,"pauseReason":"sessions_yield"}
  [1a] OK: paused status carries pauseReason=sessions_yield
  [1b] non-yielded terminal -> {"updatedAt":1780285520789,"status":"done","startedAt":1780285519789,"endedAt":1780285520789,"runtimeMs":1000,"abortedLastRun":false}
  [1b] OK: non-yielded run preserves status=done with no pauseReason
  [1c] error+yielded -> {"updatedAt":1780285520789,"status":"failed","startedAt":1780285519789,"endedAt":1780285520789,"runtimeMs":1000,"abortedLastRun":false}
  [1c] OK: error phase masks yielded marker, no pauseReason leak

  [2] seeded /var/folders/cn/.../state/agents/main/sessions/sessions.json
  [2] recovery bundle: /Users/alexm/.openclaw/openclaw/dist/main-session-restart-recovery-CirHJY3p.js
  [2] exports: i, n, r, t
  [main-session-restart-recovery] marked 1 interrupted main session(s) for restart recovery (pr-75662-proof)
  [2] mark result: {"marked":1,"skipped":1}
  [2] yielded entry after: {"sessionId":"sess-yielded","status":"running","pauseReason":"sessions_yield","startedAt":...,"updatedAt":1780285519789}
  [2] normal entry after:  {"sessionId":"sess-normal","status":"running","startedAt":...,"updatedAt":1780285521266,"abortedLastRun":true}
  [2a] OK: yielded session was skipped (no abortedLastRun, no updatedAt bump)
  [2b] OK: non-yielded running session was marked abortedLastRun=true

  === ALL PROOFS PASSED ===
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: All three scenarios pass on the deployed bundle. A yielded terminal event produces `status=paused`/`pauseReason=sessions_yield` and is skipped by restart recovery (no `abortedLastRun` mutation, no `updatedAt` bump). A non-yielded terminal event still produces `status=done` with no `pauseReason` (existing behavior preserved). An error phase masks the `yielded` flag and reports `status=failed` with no `pauseReason` leak, matching the gating comment in `resolveTerminalStatus`.
- **Targeted tests**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/main-session-restart-recovery.test.ts src/gateway/session-lifecycle-state.test.ts` -> 22 passed + 15 passed (37/37). Broader: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts src/gateway/sessions-patch.test.ts src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts` -> 172/172 passed. `node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --only=core --threads=4` -> clean.
- **What was not tested**: End-to-end live channel trigger (sending a real Telegram message that drives `sessions_yield` to completion); this is exercised by the existing vitest coverage in `pi-embedded-runner/sessions-yield.orchestration.test.ts` rather than reproduced live on rh-bot.


## Live exact-head capture (2026-06-12): found and fixed an emit-side gap

Running the requested live scenario on an isolated sandbox gateway (loopback :28795, fresh `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, Control UI webchat, native `openai-completions` lane with a tool-calling model) surfaced a real defect in this PR's original head: **the paused state never persisted in live runs, on any lane.** A store probe polling every 0.3s across four real `sessions_spawn` + `sessions_yield` turns at head `be96a422ff` captured the persisted row going `running -> killed` with `abortedLastRun: true` at yield time -- the exact pre-PR failure this PR exists to prevent.

**Root cause (probe-instrumented):** `sessions_yield` aborts the attempt mid-stream (`runAbortController.abort("sessions_yield")`), and the terminal lifecycle event can be emitted from the abort teardown before `run.ts` post-processing writes the `yielded` meta. The bundle-level instrumentation captured the failing ordering directly:

```
emitLifecycleTerminal state.yielded = undefined  aborted = false   <- event emitted first
meta.yielded set -> false                                          <- run.ts wrote 551ms later
```

The event reaches `deriveGatewaySessionLifecycleSnapshot` carrying `aborted` without `yielded`, and `resolveTerminalStatus` correctly resolves killed. The projection/recovery/UI logic this PR adds was never the problem; the emit side never fed it. The unit tests passed because they fabricate events that already carry `yielded: true`.

**Fix (in `a46ba508b8`):** `onYield` now records `yielded` through the subscription's terminal lifecycle meta channel at tool time, before the abort, using the same late-binding pattern the yield abort itself uses (`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts`). A regression test pins the handler half: an end event with `state.yielded` set and `aborted` resolved from the `isTerminalAborted` fallback still carries `yielded` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.lifecycle.test.ts`).

**Post-fix live proof, end to end (same sandbox, instrumented bundle):**

```
10:27:40.065  [PROBE] meta.yielded set -> true              (onYield records at tool time)
10:27:40.423  [PROBE] emitLifecycleTerminal state.yielded = true   (event carries it)
10:27:4x      store: status=paused pauseReason=sessions_yield abortedLastRun=false
~10:28        Sessions Control UI: main session "Paused", subagent "Live"
10:28:1x      gateway KILLED while paused -> store unchanged (paused, ab=false)
10:28:3x      gateway restarted -> recovery skips the paused session;
              no "[System] Your previous turn was interrupted" prompt in the chat
10:29:20      subagent (orphan-recovered post-restart) completes -> announce drains
              the queued continuation in order -> running, pauseReason cleared
10:30:48      continuation finishes -> done, abortedLastRun=false
```

Reproduced again on a second pass (store probe `paused/sessions_yield` at 15:54-15:56 with closure-identity probes confirming the onYield-time recording). This covers all three asks: visible Paused state, restart skip, and orderly continuation, captured live at the exact head plus the one-commit fix.

**Codex lane now covered too (was a sibling-lane gap, now fixed in this PR).** The Codex app-server harness lane emits its own terminal lifecycle event (`extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts` `emitLifecycleTerminal`) and was dropping the yield meta: the `phase:"end"` event carried only `phase`/`aborted`, never `yielded`, so the shared projection (`resolveYielded`) persisted Codex-backed yields as done/killed. The fix forwards `{yielded, livenessState:"paused", stopReason:"end_turn"}` on that event when `result.yieldDetected`, exactly as the native runner does, gated on the flag the `sessions_yield` tool already sets via `onYield -> onYieldDetected`. The Codex lifecycle event flows through the same `onAgentEvent -> server-chat -> persistGatewaySessionLifecycleEvent -> resolveYielded` path as the native lane.

Proven live on the Codex lane: a real `openai/gpt-5.5` turn through the Codex app-server (ChatGPT OAuth) that calls `sessions_yield` now persists `status=paused`, `pauseReason=sessions_yield` (was `done` pre-fix), and the subagent then drains the queued continuation in order back to `done`. An integration test (`extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.yield-lifecycle.test.ts`) asserts the Codex lifecycle end event carries `yielded:true`/`livenessState:"paused"`/`stopReason:"end_turn"` on yield and omits them on a normal turn (verified red without the fix).

Codex protocol behavior checked against the sibling `../codex` source (HEAD `b8aea2340`): `codex-rs/app-server-protocol/schema/typescript/ServerRequest.ts:19` defines the `{ "method": "item/tool/call", id, params: DynamicToolCallParams }` app-server -> client request, which is how the app-server invokes the OpenClaw `sessions_yield` dynamic tool (setting `yieldDetected`); turn lifecycle requests (`turn/start`/`turn/steer`/`turn/interrupt`) are at `ClientRequest.ts:90`. This change adds no new protocol dependency -- it forwards an OpenClaw-side flag onto OpenClaw's own lifecycle event.

target.title: Add gateway health connection telemetry
target.body:
## Summary

- Add per-client `connection` telemetry to authenticated WebSocket `health` responses.
- Track WebSocket protocol ping/pong RTT on each gateway client without mutating the cached global health snapshot.
- Document `health.connection` and add tests for stale pongs, socket close, no-first-pong staleness, and multi-client isolation.

Fixes #70230.

## Details

`health.connection` is overlaid per response and includes:

```ts
{
  connected: boolean;
  rttMs: number | null;
  lastHeartbeatAt: number | null;
}
```

The cached service-level `HealthSummary` stays shared/global. The connection block is derived from the current request client, so one client cannot see another client's RTT or heartbeat timestamp.

RTT uses WebSocket protocol ping/pong with a 5s ping cadence and a 20s stale threshold:

```ts
const CONNECTION_PING_INTERVAL_MS = 5_000;
const CONNECTION_STALE_MS = CONNECTION_PING_INTERVAL_MS * 4;
```

Before the first successful pong, `connected` can be `true` while `rttMs` and `lastHeartbeatAt` are `null`, but only during the initial stale window. If no pong is accepted within `CONNECTION_STALE_MS` from `connectedAtMs`, `connected` becomes `false`.

## Coordination with #56668

Related #56668 adds a standalone 25s WebSocket protocol ping keepalive. This PR also sends protocol pings, but uses one gateway maintenance loop so the same ping/pong path powers both keepalive and `health.connection` telemetry.

If this PR lands first, it should be the single WebSocket ping owner and #56668's standalone per-socket interval is covered. If #56668 lands first, this branch should be adjusted to reuse the existing ping owner instead of merging duplicate protocol ping loops.

## Tests

- `pnpm test src/gateway/server/connection-health.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts src/gateway/server-close.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-early.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/gateway/server.health.test.ts src/gateway/server/health-state.test.ts src/gateway/client.watchdog.test.ts src/commands/health.snapshot.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/commands/health.ts src/commands/health.types.ts src/gateway/gateway-misc.test.ts src/gateway/server-close.test.ts src/gateway/server-close.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.ts src/gateway/server-methods/health.ts src/gateway/server-methods/shared-types.ts src/gateway/server-runtime-handles.ts src/gateway/server-startup-early.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-early.ts src/gateway/server.canvas-auth.test.ts src/gateway/server.health.test.ts src/gateway/server.impl.ts src/gateway/server/connection-health.ts src/gateway/server/connection-health.test.ts src/gateway/server/ws-connection.ts src/gateway/server/ws-connection/message-handler.ts src/gateway/server/ws-types.ts docs/gateway/health.md docs/gateway/protocol.md docs/cli/health.md`
- `pnpm check:changed`

Note: localhost-binding tests were run outside the sandbox because sandboxed execution rejects `listen 127.0.0.1` with `EPERM`.

The branch preserves Google Chat thread resources through webhook context, core reply plumbing, message-tool/media delivery, validation, and tests, while also touching Google auth runtime selection, two live-test helpers, and CHANGELOG.md.

PR surface: Source +296, Tests +1374, Docs +1. Total +1671 across 27 files.

Reproducibility: yes. at source level. Current main still forwards raw Google Chat reply metadata into the Chat API thread field, and the PR discussion includes live deployed-user confirmation plus before/after screenshots of replies leaving versus staying in threads.

target.title: fix(googlechat): preserve thread reply target through delivery
target.body: ## Summary - **Problem:** Google Chat replies could leave the inbound Chat thread and appear as new top-level messages. - **Why it matters:** delayed or tool-driven assistant replies break group-thread UX when they detach from the conversation the user spoke into. - **What changed:** preserve `message.thread.name` as `MessageThreadId`, expose it to reply/message-tool threading, and normalize Google Chat outbound thread targets before they reach `thread.name`. - **What did NOT change:** no Google Chat per-thread OpenClaw session isolation; core reply plumbing remains channel-agnostic and Google Chat resource-name handling stays behind the Google Chat boundary. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #69422 - Related #39554 - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: Google Chat assistant replies from existing Chat threads should remain in the inbound thread. - Real environment tested: Linux VPS-hosted OpenClaw Google Chat runtime with service-account auth and a real Google Chat space. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: Jai sent real Google Chat threaded messages and confirmed assistant replies stayed in-thread before this PR was marked ready. - Evidence after fix: screenshot evidence is attached below: - Before: Google Chat reply appeared outside the inbound thread. - After: Google Chat reply stayed inside the inbound thread. - Scope note: typing-placeholder editing is separate follow-up behavior; this PR fixes thread placement. - Observed result after fix: live Google Chat replies stayed in-thread; local seam tests also verified natural replies, message-tool sends, and current-reply behavior use the inbound Google Chat thread resource. - What was not tested: every Google Chat account/space policy combination; typing-placeholder edit/cleanup for message-tool sends; Google Chat per-thread OpenClaw session isolation, which is out of scope. - Before evidence: prior behavior re-rooted replies as new top-level Chat messages. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: Google Chat exposes different resource shapes for messages (`spaces/.../messages/...`) and threads (`spaces/.../threads/...`), and OpenClaw previously let message-shaped values flow into thread-routing seams. - Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered legacy single-resource channels, but not a provider where current message id and current thread id are different resource names. - Contributing context: - Inbound webhook context did not populate generic `MessageThreadId` from `message.thread.name`. - The message-tool path had no inbound-thread default when the agent omitted `threadId`. - `[[reply_to_current]]` resolves to the current message id; for Google Chat that value must be converted to the inbound thread before delivery. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-plumbing.test.ts` - `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-payloads.test.ts` - `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.test.ts` - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-delivery.test.ts` - `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.webhook-routing.test.ts` - `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.reply-delivery.test.ts` - `extensions/googlechat/src/channel.test.ts` - `extensions/googlechat/src/actions.test.ts` - `extensions/googlechat/src/targets.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: Google Chat has a current message resource and a distinct current thread resource; implicit replies and message-tool sends use the thread resource, while message-shaped reply ids are rewritten or rejected before reaching the Chat API. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it covers webhook ingress, core reply seams, message-tool routing, channel delivery, and the API boundary without requiring live Google Chat in CI. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): none. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Google Chat assistant replies from an existing Chat thread now stay in that thread instead of creating a new top-level message. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: Google Chat webhook -> currentMessageId=spaces/.../messages/... -> delivery sends that as thread.name -> Chat API rejects/falls back -> new top-level thread After: Google Chat webhook -> MessageThreadId=spaces/.../threads/... -> reply/tool delivery resolves outbound thread -> Chat API receives thread.name=spaces/.../threads/... ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS local checkout; Linux VPS-hosted runtime for real Chat verification - Runtime/container: OpenClaw source checkout; local verification on exact rebased head `a617644c81dd43bb7285d3001ca6d19750e62e68`; live Google Chat verification on a Linux VPS-hosted runtime with the thread-placement patch applied - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat service-account auth - Relevant config (redacted): Google Chat enabled with group mention policy; no secret/config changes required ### Steps 1. Send a threaded Google Chat message that mentions the bot. 2. Let the assistant produce a normal final reply. 3. Exercise message-tool sends without explicit `threadId`. 4. Exercise `[[reply_to_current]]` behavior where the core seam supplies the current message id. ### Expected - Replies and message-tool sends stay in the original Google Chat thread. - Explicit thread-shaped ids are preserved. - Message-shaped or malformed thread targets never reach `thread.name` as-is. ### Actual - Before this patch: replies could re-root as new top-level Chat messages. - After this patch: live verification and local seam coverage keep replies in the inbound thread. - Scope note: typing-placeholder editing is separate follow-up behavior and is not fixed by this PR. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Screenshot evidence attached below: - Before screenshot: Google Chat reply escaped the inbound thread and appeared outside the original conversation thread. - After screenshot: Google Chat reply stayed inside the inbound thread. - Scope note: the after screenshot may still show a stale typing placeholder when the message-tool path is used; typing-placeholder edit/cleanup is not part of this PR. Verification commands run on exact rebased head `a617644c81dd43bb7285d3001ca6d19750e62e68` after the May 26 rebase: ```bash mise exec node@22.22.2 -- node scripts/docs-list.js # passed mise exec node@22.22.2 -- ./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md extensions/googlechat/src/actions.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/actions.ts extensions/googlechat/src/api.ts extensions/googlechat/src/channel.adapters.ts extensions/googlechat/src/channel.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/google-auth.runtime.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/google-auth.runtime.ts extensions/googlechat/src/monitor-reply-delivery.ts extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.reply-delivery.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.ts extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.webhook-routing.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/targets.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/thread-resource.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-payloads.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-payloads.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-delivery.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-delivery.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-payloads-base.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-plumbing.test.ts # All matched files use the correct format. mise exec node@22.22.2 -- node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/googlechat/src/actions.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/channel.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/google-auth.runtime.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.reply-delivery.test.ts   - What changed: restored a scoped Feishu compatibility SDK facade, added it to SDK export wiring, updated API baseline/docs, and added a regression contract test for the legacy export surface.

target.title: fix(skills): never wipe target skills dir on sync to prevent data los…
target.body:
…s on partial load

`syncSkillsToWorkspace` used to call `fsp.rm(targetSkillsDir)` before recopying every skill. Any transient failure inside `loadWorkspaceSkillEntries` (a filesystem hiccup, a config parse error, an agent-filter edge case) returns fewer entries than expected and the wipe step destroys skills that the recopy step cannot restore. Users report their entire skills directory disappearing after a single bad load.

Switch to a non-destructive, per-skill incremental sync:

- Only `mkdir` the target skills dir, never wipe it wholesale.
- Build a manifest of (destination dir, source entry) pairs up front, preserving the existing duplicate-basename disambiguation.
- For each entry compare source vs destination mtime via a small 2-level `getNewestMtime` helper; skip re-copying when the destination is already at least as new.
- When a copy is needed, scope the `rm` to that single destination dir so stale files inside it cannot leak into the new copy, then `cp`.
- Emit a debug summary (`manifest/copied/skipped/failed`) to make post-sync diagnostics possible without reconstructing state.

Target directories that are not part of the source manifest — for example skills the user installed directly inside a sandbox — are left untouched. Sandboxes are ephemeral, so a few leftover directories are cheap; losing real user data is not.

Add four regression tests in the existing sync suite:

- Second sync with an empty source no longer wipes previously synced skills.
- User-created skill directories survive an unrelated source sync.
- Unchanged skills are not re-copied (destination mtime preserved).
- Modified source skills are picked up and re-copied.

target.title: fix(agents): accept multi-line ANNOUNCE_SKIP on final line
target.body:
## Summary

- Fix `isAnnounceSkip()` to also accept multi-line sub-agent output where `ANNOUNCE_SKIP` appears as the final line after a newline, not just as the sole content.
- Add focused unit tests for `sessions-send-tokens.ts` covering exact, whitespace-padded, multi-line, and negative cases.
- Add an e2e regression test for the multi-line announce-skip scenario.

Closes #74071

## Problem

Sub-agents that produce a DM summary block followed by `ANNOUNCE_SKIP` on the final line had their entire summary leak into the parent session as an announcement. The strict equality check `(text ?? "").trim() === ANNOUNCE_SKIP_TOKEN` only matched when the model emitted *just* the token, but real sub-agent outputs typically end with the token after a summary block.

## Fix

`isAnnounceSkip()` now checks two conditions:
1. **Exact match** (existing): trimmed text equals the token
2. **Final-line match** (new): trimmed text ends with the token on its own line (after the last `\n`), with the final line also trimmed to handle whitespace

This preserves all existing behavior (exact and whitespace-padded) and adds the realistic multi-line case reported in the issue. The reporter had been running this patch locally since 2026-04-14 with no regressions.

## Test plan

- `pnpm test src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tokens.test.ts` — 16 new focused unit tests (all pass)
- `pnpm test src/agents/subagent-announce.test.ts` — 9 existing tests (all pass)
- `pnpm test src/agents/subagent-announce-output.test.ts` — 3 existing tests (all pass)
- `pnpm test src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts` — 15 existing tests (all pass)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: sub-agent output ending with a final-line `ANNOUNCE_SKIP` should suppress completion announcement delivery, while `ANNOUNCE_SKIP` followed by more text should not be treated as a silent sentinel.
Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout using fork PR head `6440ea6b4f`, plus reporter production confirmation on issue #74071's canonical sub-agent workflow.
Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
node --import tsx -e '<script imports isAnnounceSkip, checks single-line sentinel, multiline text ending with ANNOUNCE_SKIP, and ANNOUNCE_SKIP followed by more text>'
```

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "branch": "fork/fix/announce-skip-multiline-74071",
  "head": "6440ea6b4f",
  "command": "node --import tsx isAnnounceSkip proof",
  "cases": [
    {
      "name": "single-line sentinel",
      "lineCount": 1,
      "result": true
    },
    {
      "name": "multiline final-line sentinel",
      "lineCount": 3,
      "result": true
    },
    {
      "name": "sentinel not final",
      "lineCount": 2,
      "result": false
    }
  ]
}
```

Observed result after fix: a final-line `ANNOUNCE_SKIP` suppresses announcement, including after a summary block; a non-final sentinel does not suppress announcement. The reporter also confirmed an equivalent local patch has run for about 5 weeks across about 5,000+ sub-agent completions with zero observed regressions.
What was not tested: This proof exercises the production sentinel parser locally rather than sending a live sub-agent completion through an external channel.

## Notes

- `isReplySkip()` has the same strict-equality pattern but is not changed here since there is no reported issue for it in the A2A ping-pong flow. It could receive the same treatment in a follow-up if needed.
- Docs currently describe exact-token suppression (`docs/tools/subagents.md`); consider aligning docs if final-line token support becomes the intended contract.

target.title: fix: allow custom control UI origins
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` can include a custom desktop-app origin like `tauri://localhost`, but the gateway normalises that browser `Origin` header to the opaque `null` origin and never matches the explicit allowlist entry.
- Why it matters: Tauri/Electron-style Control UI wrappers have to fall back to wildcard origins even when operators want a specific origin allowlist.
- What changed: preserve the exact `scheme://host` origin for non-special URL schemes when the browser sends a scheme and host, then continue matching through the existing lower-case allowlist path.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): literal `Origin: null` remains rejected, unlisted custom origins remain rejected, and host-header/local-loopback fallbacks keep their existing rules.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #46520
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `new URL("tauri://localhost").origin` returns the opaque string `null`, so exact allowlist matching compares `tauri://localhost` with `null`.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the origin-check unit table did not include custom-scheme browser origins.
- Contributing context (if known): browser desktop wrappers can send non-special scheme origins even though those schemes do not have a standard web origin serialisation.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/origin-check.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: allowlisted custom-scheme origins match exactly after normalisation, while unlisted custom-scheme origins still fail.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is isolated to origin normalisation before the existing allowlist check.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Operators can set `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` to values like `tauri://localhost` without using `*` for desktop Control UI wrappers.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
tauri://localhost -> URL.origin "null" -> allowlist miss

After:
tauri://localhost -> preserved custom origin -> explicit allowlist match
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A. This keeps explicit allowlist matching required for non-wildcard custom-scheme origins.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local source checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway Control UI origin check
- Relevant config (redacted): `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins = ["tauri://localhost"]`

### Steps

1. Configure `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` with `tauri://localhost`.
2. Connect with browser `Origin: tauri://localhost`.
3. Compare against `checkBrowserOrigin` behaviour.

### Expected

- The explicit custom-scheme allowlist entry is accepted.
- Unlisted custom-scheme origins are rejected.

### Actual

- Current main sees `new URL("tauri://localhost").origin` as `null`, so the explicit allowlist entry does not match.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Local command evidence:

- `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD` passed.
- Manual Node parser check passed for `tauri://LOCALHOST`, `electron://localhost`, `https://CONTROL.example.com`, literal `null`, and malformed `not a url` cases.
- `pnpm docs:list` was attempted but blocked by Corepack registry timeout while downloading `pnpm 10.33.0` (`UND_ERR_CONNECT_TIMEOUT`).
- `pnpm test src/gateway/origin-check.test.ts` reached the repo test wrapper via cached pnpm but could not run because this worktree has no `vitest` dependency and dependency installation is blocked by registry timeouts.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: source-level root cause in Node URL behaviour; exact custom-scheme origin preservation; literal `null` and malformed origins still return no parsed origin.
- Edge cases checked: upper-case custom origin host normalises to lower case; unlisted custom scheme remains rejected by the unit table.
- What you did **not** verify: local Vitest/full changed gate, because this machine cannot install missing npm dependencies from the registry. CI should run the new test.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: custom desktop wrapper origins could be over-accepted.
  - Mitigation: this only changes the parsed origin string; explicit allowlist, wildcard, host-header fallback, and local-loopback checks remain the same, and tests cover an unlisted custom-scheme rejection.

target.title: fix(gateway/command-auth): memoize ownerAllowFrom list per raw array (#50289)
target.body:
## What

[`resolveOwnerAllowFromList`](src/auto-reply/command-auth.ts) walks `commands.ownerAllowFrom` linearly to:

1. Filter channel-scoped entries of the form `provider:value` (drop ones whose `provider` does not match the active `providerId`, strip the prefix on matches),
2. Apply the active channel plugin's `formatAllowFrom` normalizer.

The authorization pass for every inbound message calls it **twice** ([command-auth.ts:382](src/auto-reply/command-auth.ts#L382) for `configOwnerAllowFromList`, [command-auth.ts:389](src/auto-reply/command-auth.ts#L389) for `contextOwnerAllowFromList`). An operator with ~9000 owner entries paid an 18k-string-op cost per message and saw 15–27s processing latency plus repeated `lane wait exceeded` warnings, even when no command was being used.

Closes #50289.

## Why this fix

The filtered output is deterministic in the inputs (`raw array`, `providerId`, `accountId`, `plugin.id`). A `WeakMap<rawArray, Map<inner-key, result>>` cache:

- **First call** for a given config-array reference: pay the O(n) cost once.
- **All subsequent calls** in the same gateway lifetime: O(1) Map lookup.
- **Config reload**: always allocates a fresh array, so the WeakMap entry on the old array is unreachable and the new array starts uncached. **No explicit invalidation hook needed** — the GC handles it.

The inner key combines the only three inputs that change the filtered output (`providerId`, `accountId`, `plugin.id`), so different channels and accounts do not cross-pollinate cached results.

For the reporter's 9000-entry case this drops per-message authorization cost from `2 × 9000` string ops to **2 × Map lookups**.

## Tests

Added [`src/auto-reply/command-auth.owner-allow-from-memo.test.ts`](src/auto-reply/command-auth.owner-allow-from-memo.test.ts) using a small `installDiscordRegistryHooks()` fixture (same pattern the existing `command-auth.owner-default.test.ts` uses):

- Repeated calls with the same raw array return identical results and the second call is observably memoized via the new `isOwnerAllowFromListMemoizedForTest` helper.
- Different `(providerId)` keys cache independently — `discord:` and `telegram:` scoped entries route correctly to the respective providers without cross-contamination.
- A freshly-allocated raw array (config reload) starts as a cache miss; previously-cached arrays remain associated only with their original allocation.
- Empty / missing lists return `[]` without populating the cache.

The existing extensive `command-auth.owner-default.test.ts` continues to apply unchanged — the memoization is a pure performance wrapper around the same underlying filtering logic.

## Notes

- Single-area diff: `src/auto-reply/command-auth.ts` (one new module-level WeakMap, three new internal helpers including two `@internal` test exports), one new colocated test, and a one-line CHANGELOG entry under `## Unreleased` `### Fixes` with `Thanks @juan-flores077`.
- No public API change. The `resolveOwnerAllowFromList` signature is unchanged; only its internal caching is added.
- WeakMap key is the array *reference*, not its contents. Config reloads always replace the array on the parsed config object, so the cache invalidates naturally without coupling to a specific config-reload pathway.
- AI-assisted (Claude). Reviewed locally; please flag if there is a code path I missed where the same filtered list is expected to be re-walked (e.g. some test that mutates the array in place — I did not find one).

## Validation

Local CI is constrained on this machine; relying on CI for the canonical proof. I have:

- Verified by code-reading that the only callers of `resolveOwnerAllowFromList` are the two authorization-pass call sites in the same file ([line 382](src/auto-reply/command-auth.ts#L382) and [line 389](src/auto-reply/command-auth.ts#L389)) — both pass a stable `cfg.commands.ownerAllowFrom` reference and benefit equally from the cache.
- Confirmed the existing `command-auth.owner-default.test.ts` exercises the function indirectly via `resolveCommandAuthorization` and does not require the array to be re-walked per call.
- Confirmed the changelog entry is single-line and credits a contributor.

Happy to extend coverage to a perf-style benchmark or a microbenchmark if reviewers want hard numbers, but the asymptotic improvement here is what the issue asked for.

target.title: feat(gateway): add session sharing via time-limited read-only tokens
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds `sessions.createSharedToken` (WRITE scope) generating a time-limited, session-scoped read-only token
- Adds `sessions.revokeSharedToken` (WRITE scope) for explicit revocation
- Tokens are random 32-byte hex strings stored in an in-memory map with optional JSONL persistence at `{stateDir}/session-share-tokens.jsonl`
- Default TTL: 24 hours; max: 7 days; expired tokens are pruned automatically
- Auth middleware integration (restricting token holders to READ scope for their bound session) is a follow-up; token store API is ready to be wired in

## Test plan

- [ ] `sessions.createSharedToken` returns a token with correct expiry
- [ ] Token expires after TTL and is rejected
- [ ] `sessions.revokeSharedToken` invalidates token immediately
- [ ] Tokens persist across gateway restarts via JSONL backing
- [ ] `pnpm check:changed` passes

Thanks @ioodu
